name: Apep
shortDescription: Apep (also known as Apophis) is the ancient Egyptian deity of chaos, darkness, and destruction, and the archenemy of the sun god Ra. He is a monstrous serpent who dwells in the darkness and constantly attempts to devour Ra's solar barque as it travels through the underworld each night.
description: Apep, also known as Apophis, is a formidable and malevolent deity in ancient Egyptian mythology, embodying chaos, darkness, and the antithesis of Ma'at (truth, order, and cosmic balance). He is typically depicted as a colossal serpent, a monstrous embodiment of the primordial darkness that existed before creation. Apep's primary role is to relentlessly attack Ra, the sun god, each night as Ra journeys through the underworld (Duat) in his solar barque. This nightly battle is a crucial part of the Egyptian understanding of the cosmic cycle; Ra's successful passage ensures the sun will rise again, bringing light and order back to the world. Apep's attempts to devour Ra and plunge the world into eternal darkness represent the constant threat of chaos overwhelming the established order. The Egyptians believed that Apep resided deep within the earth and had a terrifying roar that could shake the underworld. Various rituals and spells were performed daily by priests to ward off Apep and ensure Ra's safe passage, including the 'Banishing of Apep' ceremony. These rituals involved destroying images of Apep, reciting spells, and performing symbolic acts of violence against the serpent, reinforcing the triumph of order over chaos. While not directly a child of any specific deity, Apep is sometimes considered a primordial being, existing before the established pantheon. He is a constant threat, a necessary evil against which the forces of good must perpetually struggle. His existence reinforces the importance of maintaining Ma'at and the vigilance required to prevent the world from descending into primordial chaos. He is the ultimate enemy of light, order, and civilization in the Egyptian worldview.

sources.title: Myths and Symbols of Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Lucia Gahlin
